narration.default: Scratch had a green Operators block with a dropdown — you could pick sqrt, sin, cos, log, abs, and more. C has the same set of math functions, but they're in a separate TOOLBOX (LIBRARY) called math.h. Just like #include <stdio.h> brings in printf and scanf, #include <math.h> brings in sqrt, pow, sin, cos, and friends. Today you'll add the math include, then use the three most common math functions: sqrt (square root), pow (raise to a power), and abs (absolute value).

Line 2: #include <math.h> — same shape as #include <stdio.h>, just a different header. Without this line, sqrt and pow below would be unknown to the compiler and you'd get a "function not declared" error. The .h files are HEADER FILES — they tell the compiler "these functions exist, here's how to call them". Most programs only #include what they need — fewer headers = faster compile times.

Line 6: sqrt(x) where x is 16.0 — returns 4.0 (because 4 × 4 = 16). sqrt takes a double (decimal number) and returns a double. Print with %.2f to show 2 decimals — sqrt(16) = 4.00. Notice we declared x as double (16.0 not 16) — math functions want decimals, not whole numbers. If you passed an int, the compiler would silently convert it, but using doubles is the cleanest style.

Line 7: pow(2, 8) — pow takes TWO arguments: the base and the exponent. pow(2, 8) means "2 to the power of 8" = 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 = 256. The result is a double. We use %.0f to show zero decimals (256 not 256.000000 — looks tidier). Try pow(10, 3) (1000), pow(2, 16) (65536), pow(3, 5) (243).

Click Run. Console: sqrt(16) = 4.00, pow(2, 8) = 256, abs(-5) = 5. abs is from stdlib (already included via stdio in this case) and works on int — returns the positive version of any number. Other math.h goodies you might explore: sin(angle), cos(angle), log(x) (natural log), log10(x) (base-10 log), floor(x) (round down), ceil(x) (round up), round(x). All take and return doubles.
